question: How did Johann Joachim Christoph Bode contribute to the spread of Laurence Sterne's works in Germany?

In this note Bode’s identity is evident in the following quotation: He says he has translated the letters “because I believe that they will be read with pleasure, and because I fancy I have a kind of vocation to give in German everything that Sterne has written, or whatever has immediate relation to his writings.” This note is dated Hamburg, September 16, 1775. In the third volume, the miscellaneous collection, there is a translator’s preface in which again Bode’s hand is evident

. The author of this translation has not been determined. Bode does not translate the word “Sentimental” in his published extracts, giving merely the English title; hence Lessing’s advice[12] concerning the rendering of the word dates probably from the latter part of the summer. The translation in the September number of the _Unterhaltungen_ also does not contain a rendering of the word. Bode’s complete translation was issued probably in October,[13] possibly late in September, 1768, and bore the imprint of the publisher Cramer in Hamburg and Bremen, but the volumes were printed at Bode’s own press and were entitled “Yoricks Empfindsame Reise durch Frankreich und Italien, aus dem Englischen übersetzt.”[14] The translator’s preface occupies twenty pages and is an important document in the story of Sterne’s popularity in Germany, since it represents the introductory battle-cry of the Sterne cult, and illustrates the attitude of cultured Germany toward the new star

. To return to Bode’s preface. With emphatic protestations, disclaiming vanity in appealing to the authority of so distinguished a friend, Bode proceeds to relate more in detail Lessing’s connection with his endeavor. He does not say that Lessing suggested the translation to him, though his account has been interpreted to mean that, and this fact has been generally accepted by the historians of literature and the biographers of Lessing.[20] The tone of Bode’s preface, however, rather implies the contrary, and no other proof of the supposition is available. What Bode does assert is merely that the name of the scholar whom he quotes as having expressed a willingness to give a part of his own life if Sterne’s literary activity might be continued, would create a favorable prepossession for his original (“ein günstiges Vorurtheil”), and that a translator is often fortunate enough if his selection of a book to translate is not censured. All this implies, on Lessing’s part, only an approval of Bode’s choice, a fact which would naturally follow from the remarkable statement of esteem in the preceding sentence. Bode says further that out of friendship for him and regard for the reader of taste, this author (Lessing), had taken the trouble to go through the whole translation, and then he adds the conventional request in such circumstances, that the errors remaining may be attributed to the translator and not to the friend. The use of the epithet “empfindsam” for “sentimental” is then the occasion for some discussion, and its source is one of the facts involved in Sterne’s German vogue which seem to have fastened themselves on the memory of literature. Bode had in the first place translated the English term by “sittlich,” a manifestly insufficient if not flatly incorrect rendering, but his friend coined the word “empfindsam” for the occasion and Bode quotes Lessing’s own words on the subject: “Bemerken Sie sodann dass sentimental ein neues Wort ist. War es Sternen erlaubt, sich ein neues Wort zu bilden, so muss es eben darum auch seinem Uebersetzer erlaubt seyn. Die Engländer hatten gar kein Adjectivum von Sentiment: wir haben von Empfindung mehr als eines, empfindlich, empfindbar, empfindungsreich, aber diese sagen alle etwas anders. Wagen Sie, empfindsam! Wenn eine mühsame Reise eine Reise heisst, bey der viel Mühe ist: so kann ja auch eine empfindsame Reise eine Reise heissen, bey der viel Empfindung war. Ich will nicht sagen, dass Sie die Analogie ganz auf ihrer Seite haben dürften. Aber was die Leser vors erste bey dem Worte noch nicht denken mögen, sie sich nach und nach dabey zu denken gewöhnen.”[21] The statement that Sterne coined the word “sentimental” is undoubtedly incorrect,[22] but no one seems to have discovered and corrected the error till Nicolai’s article on Sterne in the _Berlinische Monatsschrift_ for February, 1795, in which it is shown that the word had been used in older English novels, in “Sir Charles Grandison” indeed.[23] It may well be that, as Böttiger hints,[24] the coining of the word “empfindsam” was suggested to Lessing by Abbt’s similar formation of “empfindnisz.”[25] [Transcriber’s Note: The reference is to Böttinger, not to the present text.] The preface to this first edition of Bode’s translation of the Sentimental Journey contains, further, a sketch of Sterne’s life,[26] his character and his works. Bode relates the familiar story of the dog, but misses the point entirely in rendering “puppy” by “Geck” in Sterne’s reply, “So lang er ein Geck ist.” The watchcoat episode is narrated, and a brief account is given of Sterne’s fortunes in London with Tristram Shandy and the sermons. Allusion has already been made to the hints thrown out in this sketch relative to the reading of Sterne in Germany. A translation from Shandy of the passage descriptive of Parson Yorick serves as a portrait for Sterne. A second edition of Bode’s work was published in 1769. The preface, which is dated “Anfang des Monats Mai, 1769,” is in the main identical with the first, but has some significant additions. A word is said relative to his controversy with a critic, which is mentioned later.[27] Bode confesses further that the excellence of his work is due to Ebert and Lessing,[28] though modesty compelled his silence in the previous preface concerning the source of his aid. Bode admits that even this disclosure is prompted by the clever guess of a critic in the _Hamburgischer unpartheyischer Correspondent_,[29] who openly named Lessing as the scholar referred to in the first introduction. The addition and prominence of Ebert’s name is worthy of note, for in spite of the plural mention[30] in the appendix to the introduction, his first acknowledgment is to one friend only and there is no suggestion of another counselor. Ebert’s connection with the Bode translation has been overlooked in the distribution of influence, while the memorable coining of the new word, supplemented by Böttiger’s unsubstantiated statements, has emphasized Lessing’s service in this regard. Ebert is well-known as an intelligent and appreciative student of English literature, and as a translator, but his own works betray no trace of imitation or admiration of Sterne. The final words of this new preface promise a translation of the continuation of the Sentimental Journey; the spurious volumes of Eugenius are, of course, the ones meant here. This introduction to the second edition remains unchanged in the subsequent ones. The text of the second edition was substantially an exact reproduction of the first, but Bode allowed himself frequent minor changes of word or phrase, an alteration occurring on an average once in about three pages. Bode’s changes are in general the result of a polishing or filing process, in the interest of elegance of discourse, or accuracy of translation. Bode acknowledges that some of the corrections were those suggested by a reviewer,[31] but states that other passages criticised were allowed to stand as they were. He says further that he would have asked those friends who had helped him on his translation itself to aid him in the alterations, if distance and other conditions had allowed. question: How did Joachim Heinrich Campe address and define the concept of 'Empfindsamkeit' in his writings, especially in his publication 'Ueber Empfindsamkeit und Empfindelei in pädagogischer Hinsicht', and how was his distinction between 'Empfindsamkeit' and 'Empfindelei' reiterated in his later work 'Ueber die Reinigung und Bereicherung der deutschen Sprache'?

generated_answer:
Campe defined **Empfindsamkeit** as the **capacity for moral feeling** — “die Empfänglichkeit zu Empfindnissen, in denen etwas Sittliches … ist” — specifically joy or pain over moral good or evil. He argued that, when this sensitivity is in balance with the other faculties, especially reason, it is a **commendable and beneficial capacity**; when it is exaggerated beyond that balance, it becomes harmful .

In **“Ueber Empfindsamkeit und Empfindelei in pädagogischer Hinsicht”** (1779), he warned that **Empfindsamkeit** should not degenerate into **Empfindelei**, criticizing unbridled sentimentality and emotion that produces no real action .

In **“Ueber die Reinigung und Bereicherung der deutschen Sprache”** (1785), he repeated this distinction more clearly:  
- **Empfindsamkeit** = the properly proportioned, natural susceptibility to moral feeling.  
- **überspannte Empfindsamkeit** = the irrational, exaggerated form.  
- **Empfindelei** = “Empfindsamkeit, die sich auf eine kleinliche alberne, vernunftlose und lächerliche Weise … äussert” — sentimentality expressed in a petty, foolish, irrational, and ridiculous way, where it does not belong .
ground_truth: Joachim Heinrich Campe offered a nuanced critique of 'Empfindsamkeit' in his publication 'Ueber Empfindsamkeit und Empfindelei in pädagogischer Hinsicht', where he carefully distinguished between genuine moral sensibility and its excessive or superficial forms. He defined 'Empfindsamkeit' as a receptivity to feelings that are rooted in moral joy or pain, emphasizing that, when balanced with reason, it is a valuable and beneficent human capacity. However, he warned that when this sensibility becomes exaggerated and loses harmony with other faculties, it turns destructive, both for the individual and society. To clarify this, Campe reserved 'Empfindsamkeit' for the justly proportioned, sincere expression of feeling, while labeling its irrational, affected, or trivial manifestations as 'Empfindelei'. He further illustrated these distinctions through examples, contrasting authentic, quiet, and serious emotion with theatrical, vain, and shallow displays. This careful differentiation, first articulated in his pedagogical essay, was reiterated and expanded in his later work 'Ueber die Reinigung und Bereicherung der deutschen Sprache', where he continued to warn against the dangers of overwrought sentimentality and the epidemic of superficial feeling.

17 June

Maria of Moulines has been much on my mind today. Her sorrow, so tender and so true, has taught me again that the heart speaks most powerfully when it is least adorned. In her image I found the very spirit that moved me to write: a humanity at once humble, wounded, and noble. Her quiet suffering awakened in me that quizzical, sentimental sympathy which I have tried to carry into my own pages .

I cannot deny that my literary path has been shaped by such inspirations. In my *Die Tagereise*, later reissued as *Die Geschichte eines empfundenen Tages*, I sought to follow the trail of Yorick and Jacobi, yet I also let my own voice be heard: in my German patriotism, in my song of a German traveler, in my reflections on the poet’s place in the world, and in my tribute to Gellert at his grave . Some have called the work an imitation, even an unsuccessful one, but others have recognized the genuine feeling within it .

I have also written those smaller sentimental journeys through the visit-rooms on the holy days, and though they were issued without my name, they too were born of the same inward impulse . If I have achieved anything, it is this: to give German readers a form of feeling that is at once borrowed and my own, and to show that sympathy, when honestly felt, may become literature.
ground_truth: Diary Entry — Today, I find myself reflecting on the gentle inspiration that Maria of Moulines has bestowed upon my pen. Her story, so delicately rendered by Sterne, awakened in me a longing to capture the same tender sentiment in my own wanderings. It was her innocence and sorrow that guided my hand as I wrote of the weeping peasant-maid by the wayside, and in that moment, I felt the spirit of Yorick himself urging me to acts of kindness and reflection. My latest work, 'Die Tagereise', stands as a testament to these influences, though I am ever mindful of the fickle nature of literary fame. The reviews from the Allgemeine deutsche Bibliothek remind me that our field is in constant motion, and while Schummel’s path diverges from mine, I remain grateful for the muse that Maria has become to me.

Today I was censured by the *Allgemeine deutsche Bibliothek*, and the blow sits badly with me. They found my work wanting—an attempt at another man’s manner that they judged insufficient and tiresome . I cannot pretend the review did not sting. To be marked out as a failed imitator, as one whose book is “beneath criticism,” is a harsh fate for any author.

What troubles me more than the censure itself is Bock. I had hoped to influence him, to move him in some direction of my own making, but I have not. He has gone his own way, untouched by my intentions. That failure feels almost worse than the review, for at least criticism comes from enemies or strangers; not influencing Bock is to learn that my presence has carried no weight at all.

I am left with the humiliating certainty that I have been judged and found wanting, and with the quieter grief that I could not shape Bock as I wished.

Their review, though not as scathing as some, still wounds my pride; to be called a childish imitator, my work deemed 'beneath criticism,' is a bitter draught to swallow. I cannot help but reflect on my own earlier doubts, the sense that I have perhaps attempted the impossible in chasing another's 'Laune.' Johann Christian Bock, for all his promise, remains untouched by my efforts—my words seem to pass over him like wind through empty halls. Yet, amidst the sting of rejection, I cling to the praise bestowed by the Hamburgische Neue Zeitung, who, if only for a moment, saw genius in my labors. The world of letters is a fickle stage, but even fleeting recognition offers a balm to the wounded spirit.
